author: @sceptic33
text: just to throw in my 2cents, how about using the same metal for both current collectors, while using 2 insulated and oppositely charged electrodes running beside them to provide the steep electric field gradient to guide and accelerate the electron and ion flow? perhaps a high enough voltage could result in a photomultiplier effect; with the accelerated electron and ion currents resulting in an impact ionization cascade...   hopefully the EZ layer would do something to separate the + and - flows so reduce the amount of recombination within the cell...

author: @joeglory425
text:
I once submerged my alligator clips like you did and got an unexpected result. I came to the conclusion it was the clips themselves that were reacting. I've also had electrolyte wick up my current collectors and corrode the alligator clips in longer term experiments. Gold plating the clips would help but then you still have to have a good seal between the clip and the wire it's connected to or then you have to worry about the copper wire corroding. For this particular experiment to rule out whether the clips are affecting the outcome, you could place an extra long copper collector on one side such that it overhangs the cell and can reach up out of the beaker. To help rule out galvanic effects it can be helpful to set up a test cell that intentionally mimics a battery. If you were using copper and oxidized current collectors as Mongrel Shark suggests then as a control you could take those two strips, place a piece of paper between them and soak it with whatever solution you're using and see how it compares to the test cell.

Thanks for the video, Robert. Keep up the great work!

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: mate - the plate was 10.5cm long and only 5 cm was in the fluid - the clips weren't submerged and there was 5.5 cm of plate above the fluid level - that's would be one hell of a rate of wicking if that was responsible

author: @MongrelShark
text:
The zinc nano particles do seem to improve the performance well. As did the light. Would you expect the zinc to be consumed and need replenshing over time?

Re Al air battery.
Hmm Nice try, lol, still dosent compleatly rule out air battery 100%. At this point I would be very suprised to find thats whats going on, but cant rule it out just yet. Seen many and made a few that run on humidity. Although they mostly have high levels of Mg and a few other things not present in your experiment.
If you really want to show there is no galvanics. Just show one thats been running a load (resistor would be fine) for a week or two, should be pleanty of time to start displaying decomp in the Al. Would also rule out the inevitable claim that its now a copper-zinc galvanic cell, if it still works after a decent amount of time, and more energy has been extracted than whats in the fuel loading of the zinc.

I made a carbon (pencil lead) and Al foil cell with similar foil mass, and air humidity electrolyte. Ran a Joule Thief for a week before decomp in the foil was clear, and lasted a month before the foil was all turned to whte powder. Of course it didn't run with the foil in direct contact with the copper pos electrode. There had to be a damp paper towel seperator. Which I had doped with alum. Just foil on carbon will still destroy the foil, but its a dead short, you get no potential diference.

I still think heat oxidised copper on one side of a two copper cell would be a better way to provide direction of charge. Would work a lot better than Cu and Al. Which I'm thinking is mostly triboelectric? Wouldn't be much electronegitivity going on there.. Semiconductive p-n junction of oxidised copper would give you a higher voltage reading too.

Your probably sick of this one already and ready to show us the next amazing thing you can do with your ink. Either way thanks for the upload etc, and keep up the good work.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: fair enough - i don't think the zinc sulphide is consumed but as you point out i haven't run it for a long time only a couple of weeks

replies.author: @MongrelShark
replies.text: I would think a couple of weeks under load would start showing signs of things being consumed. Especially the Al foil. It may last longer due to the low power levels, but I would expect some kind of decay to be visible after that time.

replies.author: @stage274
replies.text:
Robert Murray-Smith  Is the the graphene you are using hydrophobic?   I was thinking of using a hydrophobic carbon, and a hydrophillic metal, to see if I can pull a potential. 
I have not watched the other vids. Where you claim, your able to make a hydrophillic Graphene sheet, in the last vid, you mentioned, you dried the ink in a mag field.. How did this effect the resistance of the Graphene? 

I wonder if the metal is making an exclusion zone also, since it is hydrophilic.   You may not be maximizing the potential.   Since on that sheet, negative charge is across the entire face?

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: stage274 when these things get small enough they are colloids really and electrostearically stabilised but i like your idea and think you should persue it as it is without a doubt not maximised

replies.author: @stage274
replies.text:
Robert Murray-Smith I kinda played around, I used conductive pencil lead, 7mm I believe, 1.5 ohm resistance, and then I used  a conductive strip of metal, not sure of what type of metal, came off a toy, where the battery would go.  The weird thing is, I got .350 volts for like 10 seconds or so, and it would drop to 250 mV... Then I used a new piece of lead, and I got the effect back .350 to .4 volts, then it would go away, and then got another new one, and the effect came back...  I did not draw amps however.   This was just paying around, the water used, was well water.. But the weird thing is, every time I used a new piece of lead. I would get .350 to .4 V potential, and it would last 10 to 20 sec or so...   

Honestly, I dont know if metal can induce an exclusion zone.  I was thinking using a metal strip, put Nafion on it. And use a razor to cut micro meter slices in it..  To harvest the negative ions.

replies.author: @stage274
replies.text: Robert Murray-Smith  What colloids ? On the face of the Graphene.  Or your methel blue mixture..  I think you have a great result here.  Pulling amps... Just amazing..
